color_semantics.TEAL: quantum reality (probability cloud, P(r) curve, mean radius, distribution)
color_semantics.CRIMSON: classical orbit picture (Bohr circle, single radius, wrong picture)

beats.narration_text: Bohr's atom has the electron on a circle — one exact radius. But the electron's most likely radius and its average radius are different numbers.

card.headline: Why the Electron Has No Orbit, Only a Cloud

beats.narration_text: In Bohr's model, the ground-state electron sits on a circle at the Bohr radius a₀ = 0.053 nm. One radius. Most probable equals average equals orbit radius.
Quantum mechanics solves hydrogen exactly. The electron is a cloud. If most probable and average are the same, there might still be an orbit — but what if they differ?
The radial probability density is P(r) = r² e^{−2r/a₀} times a constant. It starts at zero, peaks somewhere, then decays — not a spike at one radius.
Where does P(r) peak? Take the derivative, set it to zero. The most probable radius is exactly a₀. Bohr's guess of a circle lands here — but that is the only thing he got right.
The mechanism: the distribution has a tail.

card.headline: The tail pulls the average past the peak.

beats.narration_text: P(r) has a long tail at large r. The average — the integral of r times P(r) — weighs the tail. The result: ⟨r⟩ = 3a₀/2, sitting beyond the peak at a₀.
Two different numbers — a₀ for the peak, 1.5a₀ for the mean. A circle cannot do that. Two different numbers is the mathematical proof that there is no orbit.
An orbit would give one number. A cloud gives two.

The quantum cloud is spherically symmetric — no preferred direction. The 90%-probability isosurface is a sphere. No point in space is singled out as the position of the electron. There is no trajectory between measurements.

props.headline: The quantum cloud is spherically symmetric — no preferred direction. The…

beats.narration_text: Bohr got the energies right because a₀ is a real length scale in the exact solution. But the orbit is a fiction. The length scale is real. The circle is not.

P(r) = (4/a₀³)r²e^{−2r/a₀}. Most probable: dP/dr = 0 → r_peak = a₀ = 0.053 nm. Average: ⟨r⟩ = integral of r·P dr from 0 to ∞ = 3a₀/2 = 0.079 nm. The gap between them is 0.026 nm — real, measurable in the wavefunction.
What if the most probable and average radius differ? They do: a₀ for the peak, 3a₀/2 for the mean. That gap is the cloud. That gap is the end of the orbit. QUANTUM MECHANICS.

card.headline: The gap between peak and mean is the cloud.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
